title: Macaroni and cheese
subtitle: Baked pasta in a rich three-cheese béchamel with a golden breadcrumb top
excerpt: Short pasta cooked and folded into a thick béchamel made with cheddar, Gruyère, and a little Parmesan, then topped with breadcrumbs and baked until the top is golden and crunchy and the sauce underneath is still soft and creamy. The sauce must be seasoned with mustard powder and a little cayenne. Everything else is technique.
sourceNotes: Macaroni and cheese has a long American pedigree: Thomas Jefferson brought a pasta-and-cheese recipe back from Europe, and Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544) includes a baked macaroni and cheese preparation. The boxed version (Kraft, from 1937) made it a universal American household staple, though the homemade béchamel version predates and exceeds it.

content.text: The sauce is the whole dish. A thin or underseasoned béchamel produces a pale, bland result; a thick, properly seasoned one with three cheeses, mustard powder, and cayenne produces something worth eating. Make it slightly looser than you think you need. It will thicken further in the oven, and pasta absorbs liquid as it bakes. If it looks thick and tight before it goes in, it will be stiff and dry when it comes out.

Use short, sturdy pasta, elbow macaroni, penne, or rigatoni. Cook it to two minutes short of the packet time; it will finish in the oven. The breadcrumb topping should be tossed in a little butter or olive oil before scattering so it crisps rather than just drying out. The finished dish should have a top layer that is properly crunchy, not just lightly browned.
Cook the pasta and make the sauce

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Cook the {{pasta-dried}} of pasta in well-salted boiling water until 2 minutes short of the packet time. Drain and set aside. Meanwhile, melt 50 g {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour and stir for 2 minutes to form a roux. Gradually whisk in the {{whole-milk}} of whole milk in a steady stream, whisking to prevent lumps. Bring to a gentle simmer and cook, stirring, for 5 minutes until thick. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{cheddar}} of cheddar, {{gruyere}} of Gruyère, and {{parmesan}} of Parmesan until melted. Season with {{mustard-powder}} of mustard powder, {{cayenne}} of cayenne, and salt.

Fold the drained pasta into the cheese sauce until evenly coated. Pour into the baking dish. Mix the {{breadcrumbs-dried}} of breadcrumbs with the melted 20 g {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and scatter evenly over the top. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the top is golden and crunchy and the sauce is bubbling at the edges. Rest for 5 minutes before serving.

items.fix: Remove from the heat before adding the cheese; stir gently until melted, do not return to the heat or the proteins in the cheese will tighten and separate
items.cause: Cheese added to sauce that was too hot, or overcooked after cheese was added

items.fix: The sauce should be loose and pourable before the pasta is added; undercook the pasta by 2 full minutes
items.cause: Sauce was too thick before assembly, or pasta cooked too long
items.symptom: Finished dish is dry and stiff

items.fix: Toss the breadcrumbs in butter until well coated; at 200°C fan they should colour in 25 to 30 minutes
items.cause: Not enough butter on the crumbs, or oven too cool
items.symptom: Breadcrumb topping is pale and soft

items.fix: Taste the sauce before assembly. You should be able to identify the mustardy sharpness and a gentle background heat; adjust both upward if not
items.cause: Insufficient mustard powder and cayenne; these are not optional seasonings here

content.text: Macaroni and cheese has a long American story. Thomas Jefferson loved pasta and cheese after his time in France and Italy. Fannie Farmer printed a baked version at the end of the nineteenth century. Kraft Dinner arrived in 1937 and made the dish a household staple. The result sits in an odd place in food culture. It is a children's food at its simplest. It is a comfort food for adults who grew up on the boxed version. And in its proper béchamel form, it is a baked dish for a dinner table. The gap between the packet version and the homemade version is wider than for almost any other American comfort food.
